speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the-- the kapo, the woman who, in some Slavic language, said, I walked on dead bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And afterwards, a few minutes later, she says, I want you all outside, and look at those chimneys when the fire comes out. Your mothers and fathers and sisters and brothers are burning there. So you better keep quiet. Otherwise, I'm going to punish you. And we all had to go out and look at those flames. And we saw our fathers' and mothers' flames that came out from the chimney. And believe me, when we went back into the barracks, none of us slept. And it came a day-- this is backing up a little bit before we went to [INAUDIBLE]. It was Erev Yom Kippur, and there were a few Hungarian girls that started playing, and they asked the Germans if they could have candles to light the candles. And we knew we were going to fast. We didn't have any food to eat anyway, but we had something to eat before. And they lit candles and started crying. And of course, everybody was laying-- you know, and we had at that time bunk beds already. We were like maybe 10 girls in one bunk bed. But-- and everybody was crying. Then-- then one of them said, if you don't keep quiet, I'm going-- with a stick came and hitting the girls because they were crying. These were Jewish girls. But they were so overwhelmed with the job they had-- were given or taken. I don't know if anybody gave them the job or they volunteer for these jobs. But there was no reason, really no reason, for some of them to do what they were doing. And then we left. We left to-- we came to the-- to that A lager.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was it-- was it sadistic of them, of this woman to do that? Or were they trying to-- to make sure that all of you understood that it was a question of life and death and you better realize?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they knew that much life and death as we did. They were only there a few months before-- they came to Auschwitz a few months before us. They just did not go through the camps and the ghettos that we went to. They were just-- just-- this was something new to them. So they were not that hungry. They're not that much worn out. I think it was sadism. I think so. I think it was to prove-- you know, there were ignorant people, to prove that they are the-- the-- the upper hand over us. Or maybe I don't know. Maybe was a different between Polish Jews and Hungarian Jews. I don't know what the reason was that time. It-- there were probably a lot of different reasons. But I think that something came over them, you know, the power that made them-- made them feel that way.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we came to a point that they took us to, the A-lager, from that-- from that night that we were selected. We went. And we came in front of a building, and women came out like witches in little babushkas and started writing our names. And all the women that where with us started screaming, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And we asked them, why are you-- why are you crying? What happened? And they would not tell us, just said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewee
text: And all of a sudden, like from out of space, a German soldier came running, galloping, and said to the women, stop! Stop! This is a group that is going to the arbeits camp. And this was in front of the crematorium. Our names were taken for us to be lead-- led in-- into the crematorium. And this soldier running, he was sent to-- to stop and take us out. And they took us to another part of the camp, and we were sent out to the train. And we were like in a animal-- you know, like the whole thing would open, the roofs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cattle car.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. We came to Germany, and we came to [? Camitz, ?] which is, you know, the southern part of Germany. And, well, some-- most of us slept, too, because we were laying on the floor. And here, they ask us to come out, and we go into a train with plush red seats and backs. And I said to my sister, now I know how they-- how they take people to a crematorium-- you know, in-- in-- in luxury.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you already knew some black humor yourself, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went into that-- to the trains. We all sat down. Before the train start moving, we were all asleep. And we came into a little town, [INAUDIBLE], and they took us-- they took us to-- to a gate, black-- to a black gate. And I said to my sisters, well, now I know we are going to the crematorium.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the gate opens, and a woman stays in the background, a big woman, and starts screaming at that [GERMAN] that was directing, you know, the transport, why do you-- did you keep these poor children for such a time without food? I have their dinner ready, and you are kept them-- you kept them for so long.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, when we walked-- walked into that diner, we could not believe out own eyes. There were white, scrubbed tables, napkins and plates and forks and knives, and they served us cabbage with meat. Of course, everybody got very sick after that because their stomachs were so empty, you know, that--

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the date? What's the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was October, I think, the 6th or the 7th that we arrived to Oederan. It was-- there was an ammunition factory and was like a building that we lived, we stayed, you know, always on bunk beds. It was very clean. When we came in, they weighed us. Whoever did not weigh a certain amount that they had prescribed to had to stay in the-- in the place and get nourishment so we could go to the factory. And a lot of-- my youngest sister was the first one to go, and then my other-- I went to a night shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is-- is that where you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we still were not liberated. We stayed there till April the 21st. And I got very sick there. I had a gallbladder attack, and they did not-- we had a doctor [INAUDIBLE], Hungarian woman who said she's a doctor. And she told me that I have a wandering stone, a gall-- gallstone that wanders. And she-- they kept me in the dispensary for three and a half months. And the-- the Obersturmfuhrer that came from Auschwitz, and she just-- if you didn't-- if you said good morning and she didn't hear, she hit you with a fist that she broke once a girl's two teeth out. Whenever she came into this dispensary, she used to pass by my bunk bed and say, she's so young, and-- and she has to suffer so much. She pitied me. She ordered a special diet for me. They were bringing in for-- from the German kitchen-- a chairman, Aufseherin came every day with a bowl of farina cooked in milk with sugar. Well, I could not eat anymore. My stomach was completely shrunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think she pitied you specifically? She showed such cruelty to other people. Why do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the German, the fact that you could speak German? Was it that your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe it was. There were other people that spoke German-- I think somebody said-- somebody said that-- that her mother suffered from gallstones. And either it was true or not. Who knows? But it was probably an interesting thing that she must have loved her mother very much. And when they were-- it was time for us to leave when-- when the Russians came closer to-- to [? Camitz, ?] she came into dispensary, and she says, I want you to stay here, because you'll never make this march. And then I told her that I have two sisters. I says, if my sisters can stay here with me, then I stay. If not, I go on the march. And my sisters were carrying me to the train, actually carrying me. Because I could not walk anymore. And after the wall, we--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she didn't help you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she didn't help me, but she also was not against it. If I would have stayed, I would have been liberated on the 22nd of April. Because the Russians came in. And then we went on a train, and we're going back and forth and being bombarded on the train. We came to-- to Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when we were waiting, the Red Cross was that time in Theresienstadt, and they were not even interested when they were leaving. They saw 500 girls staying outside. They were not even interested to come over and ask, what are these women doing here? But we waited till they left. Then they admitted us to Theresienstadt. And I remember, like she had a girl that used to polish her shoes, her boots. And she put her-- her foot on a stump on a tree and said to the girl, now, Helenka, who is going to polish my boots from now on? You-- in two weeks, you are going to be free. But I don't know what's going to happen to me. So that Helenka said, well, Frau Obersturmfuhrer, maybe you come in with us here. She says, oh, no. I wish I could, but I can't.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at one time at the newsreel when they were showing, I saw her being latched to be hanged in-- in Bergen-Belsen. I recognize--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bergen-Belsen you saw her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bergen-Belsen I saw her. Yeah, she was latched to-- they said that she's going to be hanged. And apparently, she knew about it. She knew exactly. And it happened exactly to the two weeks the way she said it. We will be liberated. And we were liberated in Theresienstadt. And the Czech people were just marvelous. They were the nicest people that I ever confronted. They were really, really nice people, very, very fond memories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they start to take care of people? How?

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought everything we needed. They showered us with everything we needed. They opened-- they had places where they were-- took away from people that came into Theresienstadt. They opened them. They brought-- they brought sugar. They brought chocolate. They brought everything we-- we really needed. And we stayed there, and then we left. We went to a small town. And then we came back because we wanted to immigrate. So we went on the train. We went to-- on the way to Israel, but the transport was stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you were liberated, you knew when-- I mean, what-- what was the first thing that you saw? What was the first thing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you'll probably be very surprised, but we lived in a barrack. We were 14 girls in one room. And it was the end of-- of the barrack, the last [INAUDIBLE], and I heard a very heavy vehicle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were liberated by the Russians, yes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Very heavy vehicle. So I start running towards the street. And when I turned-- I came out, and I-- I saw a tank. And I recognized the Russian tank because I was in Poland on the Russian side, and I saw the tanks. So I ran, and I jumped on the stoop, and the soldier grabbed me by the hand and pulled me up and gave me a loaf of bread. In the meantime, half of the lager-- camp came out and threw me down. Everybody was stepping-- how I came out alive from that, I don't know. But when my sister [INAUDIBLE] Regina unwrapped the loaf of bread, it had mold. But they were so-- you know, they were gracious to give away-- they themselves did not have very much. And we stayed there for quite a while, and then we went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some other people came, and they brought in other people after from different camps. And it became a disaster there, because a lot, a lot of sick people came in. And they were dying every day. Somebody told us that a man that lived in the same building was there. So whatever little we had, I went-- and you couldn't go in. There were guards because the typhoid was-- fever was raging. So you couldn't go in there, but I climbed a window. And when I jumped in, I-- I fell on maybe 20 corpses. When I-- I-- I went down to bring to that man-- they said the man is sick. So whatever I had, I don't remember, a little soup or piece of bread or whatever. And he kissed me and hugged me, and he says, get out of here. Because he knew that, you know. And we stayed in Theresienstadt for quite a while. And then we went on it. And we were supposed to go to Israel. We registered to go to Israel. And there was a camp in Italy that-- from Italy, you know, they had [INAUDIBLE]. But this-- thsi transport was stopped, so they could not accommodate us, and they send us to Germany to Landsberg am Lech. That's where I met Ben. He came when we came, an American. Yeah, before we came to Landsberg, we stopped in Munich. And we all got out from the-- from the train and danced the Hora. And the depot, which was our greatest satisfaction, we-- we all sang [INAUDIBLE], and we danced the Hora. And then they took us on trucks and brought us to Landsberg am Lech. And that's where we lived. And then I met Ben, and he left later the camp and-- because we looked for my brother. We met some people that were with my brother. And this was the only brother that we had. And one said to me-- one man said to me, if anybody from this camp is alive, it would be Joseph. Because he kept us alive. When we were laying in despair on the-- on the bunk beds, he would sing for us. He had a beautiful voice. And they even told me the songs that he was singing for them.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 100%, he's alive. And we started looking in all the hospitals in all the camps, and we couldn't find him. Then one day, somebody came and told us that they found a grave in Feldafing, which was near Munich, a camp. And we went there, and I have a picture of that. We put later-- later we put a [NON-ENGLISH] for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had no money, but we had some friends that helped us to go to Munich, and we saved up. The bread that we had or a little coffee we had, we gave it to the German woman, and she made a stone for--for my brother. And then they came-- a few young men brought a little truck, and we transported it up the hill. And just a few months ago, maybe a year ago, a cousin of mine went to Germany, and I bet-- I was afraid to go to Germany to visit the grave. Because this stone meant so much to us, and we had no money. We did not have the five marks to go to Munich to check with that woman if she's making the stone. And I was afraid in case somebody did not like the writing on the stone they would destroy it. And I said, if I come there and I don't-- I won't see the stone, I would probably have a heart attack. And I never went back to Germany. I could never go back to Poland. And if anybody of Polish kids hear this video, remember it should not happen. I should not have happened. It could have been prevented. A lot of Polish people could have saved a lot of Jews. We hope that from now on, there is not going to be any prejudice, that people would love one another. And I hope that all the tapes that are going to be made from now on and young people listen to them, remember we are all human beings. We came to this country. We made a life for ourselves. We raised two children, honest people, professional people. Our son now is a doctor and a great human being. My daughter was a banker, manager, and vice president of-- of a bank. She raised three-- she raises three beautiful sons who attend the Sinai Day School. And it's nice to come on Friday and have a Shabbat dinner with them. We have a wonderful son-in-law who makes the prayers and teaches the children and helps them with their Hebrew. He studied in Technion in Israel. And he finished [INAUDIBLE], and he's making a great father and a very good husband. And our son is married to a lovely lady, Connie. And I hope when this tape comes out, that they will already have a little baby on their own. And we just hope that we gave our children the best of us that we could give them, that we raised them as good Jews. My children-- our children were in Israel. We took them. The first time we took both of them, although Estelle was a teenager, and she rebelled. And she said she wants to go on her own. But when I confronted her, I said, Estelle, for 20 years, Daddy and I want to go to Israel, and not only the money but the time could not-- did not allow us. We were just moving the business, and we could not leave it. And now we decided to go, and it's going to be the greatest, greatest joy for your father to stay at the Kotel and we all four take a picture there. And this will be a memory for life for us. And she says, no, I don't want to go. But an hour later, she came back, and she says, Mom, you're right. We are going to go, and Noah came over and kissed me and says, Mommy, we will go together. And we did. We took a picture at the Kotel, and we-- we stayed in a nice hotel with them, and we visited every place we had to go. And we-- we went with them, the Masada, to Jerusalem, and then we went to the [? Galileas. ?] We took a boat trip along the Jordan River. We went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that when you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- Noah was 14. About-- I think '71. '71-- the time we went through was fairly strict, because in Italy at that time I think was the same year when-- when they bombarded-- something happened on the the-- on the airport in Italy. And it was this very, very-- they were very strict about it. They had-- we had to open all the suitcases. And-- so-- and Estelle went back quite a few times. She was in Israel maybe four or five times. And Noah was back. When Noah graduated medical school, his wish, as Ben said-- Ben had a diamond ring, and he wanted that ring. He asked for that ring. And Ben told him that when he will graduate. And we asked him what kind of gift he would like to have for his graduation besides that. And he said a trip to Israel. And we did go to Israel that time. We stayed in the Dead Sea. And he-- we-- and he even went-- he even went scuba diving in the-- in [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- what is Noah's specialty in medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's in internal medicine. But he specializes also in diabetes and-- and pulmonary. He's very, very knowledgeable in pulmonary. He's a very-- he's a very honest-- he's a good, honest human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: So is Estelle. Estelle you don't have to ask Estelle. Estelle offers. Estelle is now very active in the Sinai Temple. With three little children, it's not easy. She finds time to take children on a trip or do something in the Temple or sell-- sell some scripts. Whatever there is to do, Estelle is there to do. You and your husband, you've been extremely active in the Jewish community. And you've contributed a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think it comes from your experience because you've survived such a difficult time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a lot-- a lot to thank God for what we have. And others did not live to have it. And also, I think they say charity begins at home, but what you see, what your parents are doing, I think you learn that you cannot live for yourself. You have to help you-- other people, too. We-- as soon as we came to Los Angeles, we joined the 1939 Club. We are very active. We directed a chair, a Holocaust chair, and-- and Dr. Goetz I'm sure you know, Samuel Goetz. He was the initiator of that. We have a-- we have a plaque. Our name is mentioned on the plaque.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm one of your students, really. I'm one of the students that has benefited from your Holocaust chair.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm happy we could help. I am very happy we could. You can see we have here-- maybe we can show it later, that is a-- the top letter [INAUDIBLE] on-- on the wall of the-- and we also belong-- I am from Lodz, so I belong to the Lodz organization. At our 40th anniversary, we made a party in the Friar's Club, and all-- all the-- instead of gifts, people made donations, and the money was sent to the Tel HaShomer Hospital in Tel Aviv. It was quite-- quite a nice sum of money that was collected that time. We also support [? Habat. ?] Every year I know they're doing a good job. Maybe some people are not agreeing with me but we saw it. We were there a few times, and we saw people that they were helping. And I think they deserved it. They should be helped. I also-- I'm also a member of the B'nai Brith Women. I'm a past president. I'm a life member. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were honored at the-- last Mother's Day you were honored.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was very fortunate to do-- We have close to 500 members. I was very honored to be chosen to be Mother of the Year. And we had-- people donated money in my honor. And the money went to the Children's Home in Israel. It was over $5,000 collected on the-- at the [INAUDIBLE] midweek luncheon. So I'm very proud of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that the Holocaust could ever happen again for-- to the Jewish people, to other people? I mean, how do you feel? Do you feel that-- that you're safe in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Safe, I cannot say 100% that I'm safe. What we confronted now with skinheads and what have you, other organizations and a lot of the Nazis. The only thing that we have to-- part of us have to stay here to sustain, to be able to help Israel. Because if we work here or our children, and if they will carry on what we are doing, because I'm very, very proud of people that survived the war, the Holocaust people. People were giving away their bread. They denied themselves, and I was among these people. I-- I was working with my husband. I denied myself once a week or once in two weeks a maid to save that money and give it to needy people. And there were a lot of people that did the same thing, and they are still doing. There are people that live today on-- on Social Security with very, very little means from other sources. But they-- it comes a time that they're ready to donate money. They're always willing. I would not say that 100%, but it's a large population of Holocaust survivors that see to it that even in this country if it's needed, a lot of organizations are supported, Meals on Wheels or what have you, the Westside Jewish Community Center. Memorial-- [INAUDIBLE] Memorial is supported by the community. I'm sure that Wiesenthal Center gets some, and a lot of other causes are given. I'm very proud to say that while I was president, I was very honored, and I have-- I have something to show that was given to the-- to the B'nai Brith [INAUDIBLE] chapter. We donate $10,000 to the-- for-- for Exodus. And it was the first time that a single organization of the B'nai Brith did it, because usually it goes into the-- into the council or the region that they-- that they donate the money to it. But I had in my mind to do it. I organized the cruise. And this money, I-- I asked for permission to take this money and-- and donate to the-- to the Exodus, which was-- they gave me the permission to do it, and I was very, very proud to be able to do it. And I hope they will continue doing it again.
